did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning algorithms, specifically CNN, ViT-B16, ViT-B32, EfficientNet V2, and ResNet-50 V2, along with ensemble methods, to classify skin lesions and predict melanoma using the HAM10000 dataset. The models were deployed on an ESP32 microcontroller to discriminate between melanoma and benign skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and deployment of deep learning models (CNN, ViT, EfficientNet, ResNet, and ensemble methods) on an ESP32 microcontroller using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 83% accuracy on ESP32) and includes methodology, results, and a conclusion, which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: Classification of Skin Lesions to Predict Melanoma using Deep Learning Algorithm on ESP32
journal: 2024 International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning system for classifying skin lesions to predict melanoma using an ESP32 microcontroller. The researchers trained and tested models, including CNN, ViT-B16, ViT-B32, EfficientNet V2, ResNet-50 V2, and ensemble methods, on the HAM10000 dataset containing over 10,000 images. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing these architectures and three ensemble techniques (weighted average, geometric mean, and average) to discriminate between melanoma and benign lesions. The CNN model achieved an accuracy of 83% when deployed on the ESP32, while ensemble methods showed higher performance in offline testing, with weighted average ensembles reaching 94% accuracy on the binary classification task. The findings demonstrate that deep learning approaches on microcontrollers can reduce computing costs and enable portable skin cancer prediction devices.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, nevus, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions) and binary diagnosis (melanoma vs. benign skin lesions).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions into seven categories using the HAM10000 dataset. It also performs binary classification to discriminate between melanoma and benign skin lesions using ensemble methods (weighted average, geometric mean, average) and individual models (CNN, ViT-B16, ViT-B32, EfficientNet V2, ResNet-50 V2). The primary task is classification, evidenced by the use of categorical cross-entropy loss, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices as evaluation metrics.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public collection of dermatoscopic images assembled by researchers from the Medical University of Vienna, Austria, to support computer-aided diagnosis methods for skin disorders. It contains images sourced from clinical, pathology, and academic settings, accompanied by metadata including patient information and dermatologist annotations.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (CNN, ViT-B16, ViT-B32, EfficientNet V2, ResNet-50 V2) and ensemble methods. The dataset was split into 70% for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing.
use.validation: Used for validation (15% of the dataset) to assess model performance during training and hyperparameter tuning.
use.testing: Used for testing (15% of the dataset) to evaluate the final performance of the models using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Matthews correlation coefficient.
use.other: Used for binary classification tasks by combining the seven classes into two categories: Melanoma vs. Benign skin lesions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the overall performance and balance between true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives, particularly for binary classification tasks.
Used to visualize and analyze the performance of classification models by showing counts of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for each class.

statistical_methods_used.method: Ensemble methods (weighted average, geometric mean, average)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to combine predictions from multiple models to enhance classification performance and accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Matthews correlation coefficient, and comparisons were made based on these metric values without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 (7-Class Classification).notes: The 'nv' (nevus) class had the highest count in the confusion matrix due to its prevalence in the dataset. Ensemble techniques generally outperformed individual algorithms in terms of accuracy and top-3 accuracy.

HAM10000 (Binary Classification: Melanom.notes: Weighted average and geometric mean ensembles achieved the highest accuracy (0.936356). The weighted average ensemble had the highest Matthews correlation coefficient (0.646739). ViT B16 had the lowest MCC among individual models.

Kaggle Dataset (Binary Classification: B.notes: Average ensemble achieved the highest accuracy (0.89697) and Matthews correlation coefficient (0.793443) on this dataset.

ESP32 Deployment.notes: The CNN model was deployed on an ESP32 microcontroller, achieving an accuracy of 83%. This demonstrates the feasibility of deep learning-based approaches on microcontrollers for portable skin cancer prediction.

architecture_used.main_model: CNN, ViT-B16, ViT-B32, EfficientNet V2, ResNet-50 V2, and ensemble methods (weighted average, geometric mean, average)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were loaded for EfficientNetV2, ViT B-16, and ViT B-32. Early layers were frozen for fine-tuning.

architecture_details.modification: Output layer modified to match the number of classes in the dataset (7 classes for HAM10000, 2 classes for binary classification).

training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Freeze early layers, train newly added layers.

classification_or_output_head.type: Binary classification model for discriminating between melanoma and benign skin lesions
classification_or_output_head.input: Characteristics extracted from the aforementioned deep learning architectures

conclusions: Deep learning-based approaches applied to microcontrollers, specifically the ESP32, can reduce computing costs and enable the development of portable devices for skin cancer prediction.
The CNN model deployed on the ESP32 microcontroller achieved an accuracy of 83%, demonstrating the feasibility of running deep learning models on edge devices for skin lesion classification.

Ensemble methods (weighted average, geometric mean, and average) generally outperformed individual deep learning architectures (CNN, ViT-B16, ViT-B32, EfficientNet V2, ResNet-50 V2) in terms of accuracy and top-3 accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset.
For binary classification of melanoma versus benign skin lesions on the HAM10000 dataset, the weighted average and geometric mean ensemble methods achieved the highest accuracy (93.64%).
On the Kaggle dataset for binary classification (benign vs. malignant), the average ensemble method achieved the highest accuracy (89.70%) and Matthews correlation coefficient (0.793).

The 'nevus' class dominates the HAM10000 dataset, which significantly impacts confusion matrices and necessitates careful evaluation of performance for this prevalent class when consolidating classes.
There is a trade-off between inference rate and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC); enhancing MCC may slow down inference, requiring a balance based on application needs (speed vs. accuracy).
The study highlights the potential of deep learning models for skin cancer detection but acknowledges room for improvement in detection accuracy, particularly regarding precision and recall.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000) and a Kaggle subset; the authors explicitly state that results may differ on other datasets, indicating a lack of external validation.

The HAM10000 dataset is heavily imbalanced, with the 'nevus' class dominating the distribution, which significantly impacts confusion matrices and necessitates careful evaluation of this prevalent class.
The number of ensemble methods tested was small, and the authors acknowledge that potentially better ensemble techniques may exist.
There is a noted trade-off between inference rate and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC); improving accuracy (MCC) may slow down predictions, requiring a balance based on application needs.
The paper acknowledges room for improvement in detection accuracy, particularly regarding precision and recall, despite the high accuracy scores reported.

The CNN model deployed on the ESP32 microcontroller achieved 83% accuracy, demonstrating feasibility for edge devices but potentially lower performance compared to offline ensemble methods (94% accuracy).
The authors used Quillbot to improve the quality of the manuscript, which is a notable procedural remark regarding the preparation of the work.
